title: Negative nonlocal and local voltages (resistances) in a quasi-one-dimensional superconducting aluminum structure
authors: V. I. Kuznetsov, O. V. Trofimov
abstract: To study a nonlocal electron transport in an aluminum superconducting quasi-one-dimensional structure, we measured negative nonlocal (local) direct current voltages in the structure in a magnetic field near the critical temperature. The structure is a normal-superconducting at $T_{cn}<T<T_{cw}$ ($T_{cn}$ and $T_{cw}$ are the critical temperatures for narrow and wide wires, respectively, making up this structure). Negative voltage arises due to a quasiparticle current flowing through the N-S interface. We plotted the experimental and theoretical temperature and magnetic-field dependences of current, resistance and voltage corresponding to the peak of negative voltage, taking into account either equilibrium or nonequilibrium superconducting fluctuations.
summary: This paper explores the phenomenon of negative local and nonlocal resistances in aluminum superconducting wires. The researchers found that when a narrow wire is in a normal state while a wider wire is superconducting, injecting current creates a quasiparticle charge imbalance. This imbalance leads to voltages with a sign opposite to the applied current. The study successfully reconciles experimental observations with theories involving both nonequilibrium charge imbalance and equilibrium superconducting fluctuations.
main_problem: The study investigates the origin of anomalous negative nonlocal and local voltages (and resistances) in a quasi-one-dimensional superconducting aluminum structure near the critical temperature.
main_result: The negative voltages are attributed to quasiparticle charge imbalance at the N-S interface, and the experimental temperature dependence is best described by incorporating equilibrium superconducting fluctuations (AL and MT corrections).
method: Low-frequency DC measurements were performed on aluminum wire networks in zero and varying magnetic fields, with experimental data compared against the nonequilibrium SBT model and equilibrium fluctuation theories.
model_or_system: A quasi-one-dimensional aluminum structure consisting of a wide superconducting wire and adjacent narrow wires, operating in a heterogeneous normal-superconducting (N-S) regime where $T_{cn} < T < T_{cw}$.
key_observables: Nonlocal voltage ($V_{NL}$), local voltage ($V_L$), nonlocal resistance ($R_{NL}$), local resistance ($R_L$), and critical currents ($I_c$).
important_parameters: Critical temperatures ($T_c \approx 1.486$ K), coherence length ($\xi$), quasiparticle diffusion length ($\lambda_Q$), and magnetic field ($B$).
main_assumptions: The structure is treated as quasi-one-dimensional ($d \ll w_n < w_w < 2\xi(T)$) and the aluminum is in the dirty superconductor regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the resistive transition and structure sketch; Figure 2 displays $V(I)$ curves highlighting negative voltage peaks; Figure 3 shows nonlocal $V(I)$ curves; Figure 4 shows temperature dependence of negative resistances; Figure 5 shows critical current temperature dependence; Figure 9 shows critical current vs. magnetic field; Figure 10 shows negative resistance vs. magnetic field.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and the phenomenon of negative voltage, describes the measurement of resistive transitions and voltage peaks, analyzes the temperature and magnetic field dependence of resistances and critical currents, and concludes by comparing experimental results with nonequilibrium and equilibrium fluctuation models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (Color online) Resistive N-S transition R0(T) as a function of T at I = 0.11 µA. Circles are experimental data. The solid and dash-dotted lines are ﬁtting functions R1(T) and R2(T), taking into account one σ1AL correction to the conductivity and two σ1AL and σ1AMT corrections, respectively. Inset: a sketch of the structure.

Figure 3: (Color online) Lines 1-12 are nonlocal VNL(I) curves measured ac- cording to the measurement circuit (I - 3-2, V - 1-4) in zero ﬁeld at diﬀerent T, respectively: 1 - 1.491 K, 2 - 1.486 K, 3 - 1.484 K, 4 - 1.481 K, 5 - 1.476 K, 6 - 1.472 K, 7 - 1.468 K, 8 - 1.462 K, 9 - 1.458 K, 10 - 1.456 K, 11 - 1.453 K, 12 - 1.452 K. Lines 1-6 and 8-12 are displaced down and up vertically, respectively, relative to line 7.

Figure 2: (Color online) Line 1 - nonlocal VNL(I) curve recorded according to the measurement circuit (I - 3-2, V - 1-4). Lines 2-4 - local VL(I) curves recorded according to diﬀerent measurement circuits (line 2: I - 3-1, V - 2-4; 3: I - 2-1, V - 3-4; 4: I - 3-4, V - 2-1). Inset at the top: VL(I) is a curve taken in a larger range of currents and corresponding to line 2. All curves were taken at T = 1.462 K and B = 0. Inset at the bottom: a sketch of the structure.

Figure 4: (Color online) Measured negative nonlocal (circles) and local (squares) resistances as functions of temperature at B = 0. Lines 1 and 2 are ﬁts of nonlocal resistance in the range of 1.453 - 1.482 K by the functions RNL1(T) and RNL2(T) taking into account one σ1AL correction to conductivity and two σ1AL and σ1AMT corrections, respectively. Line 3 is a ﬁt of nonlocal resistance in the range of 1.481 - 1.491 K by the linear function RNL3(T). Line 4 is a ﬁt of local resistance (squares) in the range of 1.460 - 1.484 K by the func- tion RL4(T), which takes into account two σ1AL and σ1AMT corrections. Inset below: the dashed and solid lines show the calculated temperature-dependent...

Figure 5: (Color online) Measured nonlocal (circles) and local (squares) critical currents as functions of T in B = 0. Lines 1 and 2 are linear ﬁts of the nonlocal critical current (circles) in two ranges of 1.455 −1.477 K and 1.475 −1.491 K by the functions IcNL1(T) and IcNL2(T), respectively. Line 3 is a linear ﬁt of the local critical current (squares) by the IcL3(T) function in the range of 1.460 − 1.484 K. Inset: a sketch of the structure.

Figure 7: (Color online) Nonlocal voltages VNL1(I) (solid line 1) and VNL2(I) (dash-dotted line 2) as functions of I at T = 1.459 K in the zero ﬁeld, recorded in the right and left parts of the structure with two measurement circuits (I - 3 - 2, V - 1 - 4) and (I - 4 - 1, V - 2 - 3), respectively. Inset: a sketch of the structure.

Figure 6: (Color online) Measured maximum (peak) values of negative nonlo- cal (circles) and local (squares) voltages as functions of T in B = 0. Lines 1 and 2 are the ﬁts of the nonlocal voltage (circles) in the range of 1.453−1.478 K by two functions VNL1(T) and VNL2(T), taking into account σ1AL correction to the conductivity and two σ1AL and σ1AMT corrections, respectively. Line 3 is the ﬁt of the other part of the nonlocal voltage (circles) in the range of 1.481−1.491 K by the function VNL3(T) = RNL3(T)IcNL2(T). Line 4 is the ﬁt of the local resis- tance (squares) in the range of 1.460 −1.484 K by the function VL4(T), taking into account both σ1AL and σ1AMT corrections. Top inset:...

Figure 8: (Color online) Measured nonlocal VNL(I) curves (I - 3 - 2; V - 1 - 4) at T = 1.468 K in diﬀerent ﬁelds B for lines 1 - 7. Line 1 - B = 0, 2 - 8.3, 3 - 12.3, 4 - 20.6, 5 - 25.7, 6 - 30.0, 7 - 48.4 G. Lines 1-2 and 4-7 are displaced up and down vertically relative to line 3, respectively. Inset: a sketch of the structure. The arrow indicates the direction of the applied direct current.

Figure 9: (Color online) Experimental nonlocal critical current as a function of the ﬁeld B at T1 = 1.471 K (closed triangles) and T2 = 1.468 K (open trian- gles). Lines 1 and 2 are the ﬁts of the nonlocal critical current (closed and open triangles) by the functions IcNL1(T1, B) and IcNL2(T2, B), respectively. Inset: a sketch of the structure.

Figure 10: (Color online) Measured negative nonlocal resistance as a function of the ﬁeld B at T1 = 1.471 K (closed circles) and T2 = 1.468 K (open circles). Lines 1 and 2 are the ﬁts of negative nonlocal resistance (closed and open cir- cles) by the functions RNL1(T1, B) and RNL2(T2, B), respectively. Inset upper: a sketch of the structure. Inset below: dash-dotted and solid lines are magnetic- ﬁeld dependences of the resistance R(x0, T2, B) and the quasiparticle diﬀusion length λQ(T2, B) at T2, calculated within the framework of the nonequilibrium SBT model, respectively.
